creator: junchoon@dec.sakura.ne.jp
text:
(In reply to Michael from comment #14)
Now I'm much more confused reading from top.
You mangled Linux world and FreeBSD world on writing.

Linux version of drivers should not built on FreeBSD unless ported for LinuxKPI support, but Linux versions of drivers doesn't. Instead, there's x11/linux-nvidia-libs[-*] that installs non-driver part (libraries and configurations and so on) of pre-built (by upstream) Linux driver package, that runs on Linuxulator and FreeBSD version of driver. My first interpretation was building this (in this case, x11/linux-nvidia-libs-390), but reading precisely it was not.

And upstream FreeBSD versions of driver package builds only on stable and releng AT THE TIME THE DRIVER PACKAGE IS STARTED TO BE PROVIDED. There's checks for OS version that refuses to build on newer major versions and ports applies patch to kill the check to allow builds on newer major versions (main [aka Current] branch at the moment).

Lastly, I've never know of inxi (by googling, found it for Linux and found there's ports sysutils/inxi just now), but the output indicates nvidia driver 340 series is loaded and running.

Looking into upstream driver archive [1], supports for GT710 is dropped at 495.46, which is the oldest provided version beyonds 470 series. [2]

We can nothing to do with dropped product, thus, legacy drivers exist to help users of dropped products from newer series of drivers.

With staging area things, drivers and kernels are becoming larger and larger as versions goes by, thus, large drivers like nvidia.ko easily overflows, thus, truncated. This makes the truncated drivers (kernel modules) to be of "undefined behavior". And as the header part of the modules are loaded into staging area, kernel thinks it's pre-loaded before kernel itself starts, although it's false.
So loading non-essential-for-boot modules like x11/nvidia-driver are strongly suggested NOT to load via /boot/loader.conf but via kld_list variable in /etc/rc.conf[.local]. This way, kernel can know unsane loads.

And lastly, AFAIK, x11-drivers/xf86-video-nv is far behind x11/nvidia-driver[-*].


[1] https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/drivers/unix/freebsd-x64-archive/

[2] https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/drivers/details/184250/
